title: Exodus 34
subtitle: New Tablets; God Proclaims His Name; Covenant Renewed; Moses' Radiant Face

sections.header: Verses 1–9 — The New Tablets; God Proclaims His Name

notes.note: MacArthur: The LORD, the LORD, the compassionate and gracious God, slow to anger, abounding in love and faithfulness... The thirteen attributes are cited or echoed over a dozen times throughout the OT (Num 14:18; Neh 9:17; Ps 86:15; Joel 2:13; Jonah 4:2).

MacArthur: Moses's prayer acknowledges the sin (stiff-necked), appeals to divine grace (if I have found favour), and grounds the request in covenant identity (take us as your inheritance). A masterpiece of covenant intercession.

heb.paragraph: V.6-7: "The LORD, the LORD, the compassionate (raḥûm) and gracious (ḥannûn) God, slow to anger, abounding in love and faithfulness, maintaining love to thousands, and forgiving wickedness, rebellion and sin." This divine self-proclamation — the Thirteen Attributes of Mercy in Jewish tradition — is the most cited divine self-description in the OT. It is quoted in Num 14:18; Neh 9:17; Ps 86:15; 103:8; Joel 2:13; Jonah 4:2. It is the theological heart of what YHWH means.

V.10,27: "I am making a covenant (berît) with you." After the golden calf rupture, God renews the covenant — re-granting what had been forfeited by idolatry. The renewal is not a lesser covenant but the same one, its terms restated and the tablets re-cut by Moses's own hand (v.28). The renewal of covenant after catastrophic betrayal is itself the most powerful statement of divine grace: the relationship survives the worst failure the people can manage.

hist.historical: The divine self-proclamation (vv. 6–7) — 'The LORD, the LORD, compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in love and faithfulness' — became the most quoted creed in the Hebrew Bible (Num 14:18; Neh 9:17; Ps 86:15; Joel 2:13; Jonah 4:2). The thirteen attributes of mercy enumerated here became foundational in Jewish liturgical theology, recited on Yom Kippur and fast days. This is arguably the most important theological statement in the Old Testament — God defining his own character.
hist.context: The thirteen attributes of vv.6–7 are the answer to Moses' request in 33:18 ("show me your glory"). God's glory is not a visual spectacle but a character disclosure. The attributes are cited repeatedly throughout the OT: Num 14:18, Neh 9:17, Ps 86:15, 103:8, 145:8, Joel 2:13, Jon 4:2, Nah 1:3. They became the foundation of Israel's theology and liturgy — the character of God that makes covenant possible despite human faithlessness.

refs.note: "The Lord is compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in love" — a direct quotation of Exod 34:6.
"Full of grace and truth" — the NT echo of Exod 34:6's "abounding in love (ḥesed) and faithfulness (ʾemet)."
Jonah's complaint about God: "I knew that you are a gracious and compassionate God, slow to anger and abounding in love." He cites Exod 34:6 as the problem — God is too merciful.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The thirteen attributes (middot) became central in Jewish liturgy. Sarna notes they are recited on the High Holy Days — the community confessing sin before the God most fully characterised by mercy. The list moves from mercy (five attributes) to justice (two), with mercy predominating.

The radiance of Moses' face uses qaran (to send out beams). Jerome's Vulgate mistranslated it cornuta (horned), producing Michelangelo's horned Moses. The correct reading is 'shone' — reflected glory from extended divine encounter.

The name-proclamation answers Moses' request to see the divine glory (33:18). Alter notes the shift: visual request answered verbally. The divine character is most truly revealed through speech, not image. The thirteen attributes are the content of knowing God.

Moses' veil is worn outside the encounter (when speaking to the people) but removed inside it (when speaking to God). Mediated relationship requires veiling; immediate relationship requires unveiling.
Calvin: 'This is the most complete summary of divine perfections found in Scripture. God describes himself according to our capacity, not his nature; yet so that we may truly know him.'

Calvin on 'not leaving the guilty unpunished': 'God is so merciful as yet to be a just judge. There is a method by which God can forgive sins without injury to his justice — by transferring the punishment to Christ.' The cross resolves the tension.

The NET note traces the thirteen attributes through their OT citations (Num 14:18; Neh 9:17; Ps 86:15; 103:8; Joel 2:13; Jonah 4:2). These consistent reuses establish 34:6–7 as the OT's foundational statement of divine character assumed by all subsequent divine characterisation.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The name-proclamation answers Moses' request to see the divine glory (33:18). Alter notes the shift: visual request answered verbally. The divine character is most truly revealed through speech, not image. The thirteen attributes are the content of knowing God.

Moses' veil is worn outside the encounter (when speaking to the people) but removed inside it (when speaking to God). Mediated relationship requires veiling; immediate relationship requires unveiling.
Calvin: 'This is the most complete summary of divine perfections found in Scripture. God describes himself according to our capacity, not his nature; yet so that we may truly know him.'

Calvin on 'not leaving the guilty unpunished': 'God is so merciful as yet to be a just judge. There is a method by which God can forgive sins without injury to his justice — by transferring the punishment to Christ.' The cross resolves the tension.

Calvin on 'not leaving the guilty unpunished': 'God is so merciful as yet to be a just judge. There is a method by which God can forgive sins without injury to his justice — by transferring the punishment to Christ.' The cross resolves the tension.

The NET note traces the thirteen attributes through their OT citations (Num 14:18; Neh 9:17; Ps 86:15; 103:8; Joel 2:13; Jonah 4:2). These consistent reuses establish 34:6–7 as the OT's foundational statement of divine character assumed by all subsequent divine characterisation.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note traces the thirteen attributes through their OT citations (Num 14:18; Neh 9:17; Ps 86:15; 103:8; Joel 2:13; Jonah 4:2). These consistent reuses establish 34:6–7 as the OT's foundational statement of divine character assumed by all subsequent divine characterisation.

The NET note on the thirteen attributes of God traces each through the OT: merciful (rachum), gracious (channun), slow to anger (erekh apayim), abounding in steadfast love (hesed), faithfulness (emet), maintaining love (notser hesed), forgiving wickedness, rebellion, and sin (nasa avon), yet not leaving the guilty unpunished (venaqeh lo yenaqeh). The note discusses each term's semantic range.

sections.header: Verses 10–28 — The Covenant Renewed; the Forty Days

poi.name: Summit of Mount Sinai
poi.text: Moses spends another forty days on the mountain — the second of three Sinai ascents. The physical location is the same; the spiritual condition has changed: first the law was given in covenant, then broken, now restored. Mount Sinai is both the place of covenant and the place of covenant renewal.

tl.name: Second 40 days — covenant renewed — this passage
tl.text: The second 40-day period on the mountain mirrors the first. New tablets replace the broken ones. The divine name proclamation (34:6-7) is the covenant's theological heart, given in the context of forgiveness after failure.

tl.name: Tabernacle erected on 1st day of 1st month, 2nd year

notes.note: MacArthur: Write down these words, for in accordance with these words I have made a covenant with you and with Israel. The written covenant is the authoritative document. Both the oral proclamation of the divine character and the written tablets are essential: character and commandment.

MacArthur: Moses was there with the LORD forty days and forty nights without eating bread or drinking water. Moses's physical dependence on the divine presence during both Sinai periods is the OT's most radical expression of living by divine word, not bread alone (Deut 8:3).

heb.paragraph: V.29-35: "Moses did not know that the skin of his face was shining (qāran)." The verb qāran (to send out rays, to shine) shares a root with qeren (horn). Jerome's Vulgate translated it as "his face had horns" — giving rise to Michelangelo's famous horned Moses. The Hebrew clearly means radiance: Moses's face reflected the divine glory he had encountered. Paul uses this passage in 2 Cor 3:7-18 to contrast the fading glory of the old covenant with the surpassing, unfading glory of the new.

V.33-35: Moses wore a veil over his shining face between the times of speaking with God and with Israel. Paul's allegory in 2 Cor 3:13-18 is careful: Moses veiled his face "so that the Israelites might not see the end of what was passing away." The veil concealed the fading. Christ removes the veil: "whenever anyone turns to the Lord, the veil is taken away." The glory of the new covenant is not hidden but transforming.

hist.historical: The covenant-renewal stipulations (vv. 10–26) overlap with but are not identical to the Exodus 23 version, suggesting a liturgical re-presentation adapted to the post-calf context. The prohibition against treaties with Canaanite peoples and the command to destroy their cult installations (asherah poles, sacred stones) reflect the zero-tolerance policy toward syncretism that the golden calf incident had just demonstrated was necessary. The forty-day fast on the mountain parallels Moses' first ascent — a deliberate literary and theological framing.
hist.context: The renewed covenant (vv.10–28) focuses primarily on the dangers of Canaanite religious influence — the very threat that produced the golden calf. The lessons of ch.32 are immediately applied: no treaties, no intermarriage, no Asherah poles, no other gods. God's jealousy (v.14: "the Lord, whose name is Jealous") is the appropriate response to covenant love that cannot be shared. The three annual festivals (Unleavened Bread, Weeks, Ingathering) frame Israel's calendar with covenant remembrance.

refs.note: Jesus fasts forty days and forty nights — echoing Moses' forty days in Exod 34:28.
"The Lord your God is a consuming fire, a jealous God." Moses later cites this chapter as the ground for the warning against Canaanite religion.
"Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers" — the NT application of the treaty prohibition of Exod 34:12.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moses' face qaran (sent out rays/shone). Sarna notes Jerome's Vulgate mistranslated it cornuta (horned), producing Michelangelo's horned Moses. The correct reading is 'shone' — reflected divine glory from extended encounter.

The radiance of Moses' face after extended divine encounter is the physical sign of theophanic communion. Sarna notes the word qaran (shone, sent out rays) appears only three times in the OT — all describing Moses at Sinai. The unique vocabulary marks the experience as unique: no other prophet receives the same extended, face-to-face divine encounter.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moses' veil is worn outside the encounter (speaking to the people) but removed inside it (speaking to God). Mediated relationship requires veiling; immediate relationship requires unveiling.

Moses' radiant face after his forty-day encounter is the physical evidence that he has been in the divine presence. Alter notes the community's fear at the radiance — they cannot approach until Aaron reassures them — establishes the mediating function of Moses: the glory that would overwhelm the community is borne by their representative.

Calvin: 'The face of Moses was not always shining, but only when he came forth from speaking with God.' The glory is borrowed, not inherent — reflective of the divine presence, not intrinsic to Moses.

Calvin: Moses did not know his face was shining — the glory was not self-aware or self-displayed but was the unconscious overflow of divine encounter. This humility is itself significant: the person most marked by God's glory is the last to claim it for themselves.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on forty days and nights without bread or water notes the parallel with Elijah (1 Kgs 19:8) and Jesus' temptation (Matt 4:2). Divine sustenance replaces physical sustenance — the word of God is more nourishing than food.

The NET note on the Vulgateqornuta (horned) mistranslation discusses its artistic legacy — Michelangelo's Moses has horns because Jerome misread qaran as qeren. The note explains the correct reading: qaran (to send out rays, to shine) from qeren (horn/ray) — the semantic connection is through the radiating shape, not the bovine feature.

sections.header: Verses 29–35 — Moses' Radiant Face; the Veil

notes.note: MacArthur: Moses came down from Mount Sinai unaware that his face was radiant. The involuntary radiance of Moses's face is the physical consequence of sustained divine encounter. Paul (2 Cor 3:7-18) develops this as the contrast between the fading glory of the old covenant and the surpassing, permanent glory of the new.

MacArthur: The radiant face of Moses is unconscious — he does not know his face is shining. The glory is not manufactured display but the overflow of extended divine encounter. The veil is worn not to protect Moses but to protect Israel from the fading of the glory, which Paul (2 Cor 3) interprets as the transience of the Mosaic administration compared to the permanent glory of the new covenant.

heb.paragraph: V.22-24: The three pilgrimage festivals are re-stated after the covenant renewal: Unleavened Bread, Weeks (Shavuot), and Ingathering. Their placement here after the golden calf and covenant renewal underscores that worship — structured, seasonal, embodied encounter — is the purpose of liberation. The Exodus was not an end in itself but the means to a worshipping people. The festivals are the recurring answer to the question "why did God bring you out of Egypt?"

V.25: "Do not offer the blood of a sacrifice to me along with anything containing yeast." The prohibition on leaven at sacrifice time (ḥāg hammasôt) is not food law but purity law: leaven represents contamination, puffing up, moral inflation. Paul's metaphor in 1 Cor 5:6-8 draws on this: "A little yeast leavens the whole batch of dough. Get rid of the old yeast... for Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed."

hist.historical: Moses' radiant face (qāran, from the root qrn, 'horn/ray') is the origin of the medieval artistic convention (through the Vulgate's cornuta, 'horned') of depicting Moses with horns. The phenomenon of reflected divine glory has parallels in Egyptian texts describing the pharaoh's face radiating divine light after encountering the gods. The veil (masweh) Moses wears thereafter when not speaking God's words establishes a visible marker of mediated revelation. Paul later interprets this veil typologically in 2 Corinthians 3:7–18.
hist.context: The shining face is the physical consequence of sustained encounter with God's glory. Moses did not know his face was radiant (v.29) — the transformation was not self-generated but received. This is the pattern of genuine holiness: it is not performed but reflected. The people who encounter the glory of God are changed by it without knowing it. 2 Cor 3:18 promises all believers this transformation: "we all, who with unveiled faces contemplate the Lord's glory, are being transformed into his image with ever-increasing glory."

refs.note: Paul's extended meditation: the fading glory of Moses' face → the unfading glory of the new covenant → "we all... are being transformed into his image with ever-increasing glory."
The Transfiguration: "His face shone like the sun." Jesus on the mountain of transfiguration fulfils what Moses on Sinai foreshadowed.
The glorified Christ: "His face was like the sun shining in all its brilliance." The ultimate radiance beyond Moses' reflected glory.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The veil (masveh) that Moses puts on after speaking to the people is unique in the OT. Sarna notes it creates a three-mode pattern: bare face (in God's presence), radiant face (descending with the word), veiled face (in regular daily ministry).

The veil's three-state pattern (removed before God, radiating before the people, covered in ordinary life) encodes the covenant's graduated-revelation structure. Sarna notes the veil is not concealment of what Moses lacks but protection of what the people cannot bear. The mediation is pastoral, not hierarchical.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The veil and unveiling pattern enacts the theology of mediated revelation: the people cannot bear the full brilliance of the divine word as Moses receives it; the veil makes the mediated word bearable.

The veil's use creates a visual theology of mediation: the people see Moses' radiant face when he transmits the divine word, then the veil goes on for ordinary interaction. Alter notes the ritual establishes that the divine glory is not permanently visible — it must be re-received in each new encounter and communicated at the moment of transmission.

Calvin on the veil: 'Moses covered his face, lest the Israelites, dazzled by its brightness, should be unable to endure the sight.' The veil is pastoral accommodation — protecting the people from what they cannot yet bear.

Calvin: The veil that Moses wore was a sign that the full glory of the law was not yet revealed — the veil expressed the same limitation that Paul describes in 2 Cor 3:13. What the veil concealed was the fading of the glory: the impermanence of the old covenant's administration. Christ removes the veil entirely.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Paul's use of this passage in 2 Cor 3:7–18 contrasts the fading glory of the old covenant with the permanent glory of the new. The NET note discusses Paul's Christological interpretation: the veil over Moses' face becomes the veil over Israel's heart (2 Cor 3:15).

The NET note on Paul's use of the veil passage in 2 Cor 3:7-18 discusses whether Paul's interpretation ('so that Israel might not see the end of what was passing away') is the primary meaning of the Exodus text or a typological extension. The note surveys the scholarly debate and identifies the Christological development as legitimate biblical-theological extension.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: LORD is a key figure in Scripture Walkthrough.

ppl.name: Israel
ppl.text: They became the foundation of Israel's theology and liturgy — the character of God that makes co  Context  The renewed covenant (vv…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationESV"The Lord, the Lord, a God merciful and gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness, keeping steadfast love for thousands, forgiving iniquity and transgression and sin, but who will by no means clear the guilty."NIV"The Lord, the Lord, the compassionate and gracious God, slow to anger, abounding in love and faithfulness, maintaining love to thousands, and forgiving wickedness, rebellion and sin. Yet he does not leave the guilty unpunished."

src.title: Thirteen Attributes in Judaism
src.quote: The thirteen divine attributes (raḥûm, ḥannûn, etc.) of Exod 34:6–7 became the most quoted passage in Jewish liturgy. They are recited on Yom Kippur and on every fast day. Every petition for forgiveness in Jewish prayer returns to this passage as its foundation.
src.note: The theological centrality of these attributes in Judaism demonstrates their formative power in OT theology.

src.title: Paul's Midrash on the Veil
src.quote: 2 Corinthians 3:7–18 is Paul's sustained midrashic interpretation of Exod 34:29–35. His reading is not arbitrary; it follows rabbinic interpretive patterns while applying them to Christ.
src.note: 2 Corinthians 3 is the most sustained NT engagement with any single Exodus passage.

rec.text: Paul's most extended engagement with a single Exodus passage — the veil of Moses as the type of the veil over Israel's hearts, removed in Christ; the transformation of believers with unveiled faces.

Moses appears at the Transfiguration alongside Elijah — the two great Sinai figures. Jesus' face shines like the sun, surpassing the reflected glory of Moses' face. The Transfiguration is the Sinai theophany fulfilled and exceeded.

rows.label: The Thirteen Attributes: The Name Proclaimed
rows.text: The LORD, the LORD, the compassionate and gracious God, slow to anger, abounding in love and faithfulness... The covenant name is interpreted by the covenant character: thirteen attributes define what YHWH means in covenant relationship.

rows.label: The Radiant Face: Consequences of Extended Divine Encounter
rows.text: Moses came down from the mountain unaware that his face was radiant. The unconscious radiance of prolonged divine encounter is the opposite of self-display. The glory is not claimed; it overflows.

hebtext.note: V.6-7: "The LORD, the LORD, the compassionate (raḥûm) and gracious (ḥannûn) God, slow to anger, abounding in love and faithfulness, maintaining love to thousands, and forgiving wickedness, rebellion and sin." This divine self-proclamation — the Thirteen Attributes of Mercy in Jewish tradition — is the most cited divine self-description in the OT. It is quoted in Num 14:18; Neh 9:17; Ps 86:15; 103:8; Joel 2:13; Jonah 4:2. It is the theological heart of what YHWH means.

V.10,27: "I am making a covenant (berît) with you." After the golden calf rupture, God renews the covenant — re-granting what had been forfeited by idolatry. The renewal is not a lesser covenant but the same one, its terms restated and the tablets re-cut by Moses's own hand (v.28). The renewal of covenant after catastrophic betrayal is itself the most powerful statement of divine grace: the relationship survives the worst failure the people can manage.

V.29-35: "Moses did not know that the skin of his face was shining (qāran)." The verb qāran (to send out rays, to shine) shares a root with qeren (horn). Jerome's Vulgate translated it as "his face had horns" — giving rise to Michelangelo's famous horned Moses. The Hebrew clearly means radiance: Moses's face reflected the divine glory he had encountered. Paul uses this passage in 2 Cor 3:7-18 to contrast the fading glory of the old covenant with the surpassing, unfading glory of the new.

V.33-35: Moses wore a veil over his shining face between the times of speaking with God and with Israel. Paul's allegory in 2 Cor 3:13-18 is careful: Moses veiled his face "so that the Israelites might not see the end of what was passing away." The veil concealed the fading. Christ removes the veil: "whenever anyone turns to the Lord, the veil is taken away." The glory of the new covenant is not hidden but transforming.

V.22-24: The three pilgrimage festivals are re-stated after the covenant renewal: Unleavened Bread, Weeks (Shavuot), and Ingathering. Their placement here after the golden calf and covenant renewal underscores that worship — structured, seasonal, embodied encounter — is the purpose of liberation. The Exodus was not an end in itself but the means to a worshipping people. The festivals are the recurring answer to the question "why did God bring you out of Egypt?"

V.25: "Do not offer the blood of a sacrifice to me along with anything containing yeast." The prohibition on leaven at sacrifice time (ḥāg hammasôt) is not food law but purity law: leaven represents contamination, puffing up, moral inflation. Paul's metaphor in 1 Cor 5:6-8 draws on this: "A little yeast leavens the whole batch of dough. Get rid of the old yeast... for Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed."

thread.text: "The Lord is compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in love" — a direct quotation of Exod 34:6.
"Full of grace and truth" — the NT echo of Exod 34:6's "abounding in love (ḥesed) and faithfulness (ʾemet)."
Jonah's complaint about God: "I knew that you are a gracious and compassionate God, slow to anger and abounding in love." He cites Exod 34:6 as the problem — God is too merciful.
Jesus fasts forty days and forty nights — echoing Moses' forty days in Exod 34:28.
"The Lord your God is a consuming fire, a jealous God." Moses later cites this chapter as the ground for the warning against Canaanite religion.
"Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers" — the NT application of the treaty prohibition of Exod 34:12.

tx.title: The divine attributes formula
tx.content: MT's 'merciful and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness' is one of the most quoted OT texts within the OT itself (Num 14:18, Ps 86:15, Joel 2:13, Jonah 4:2, etc.). The LXX translates each attribute consistently across occurrences. No textual variant exists in any witness.
tx.note: The formula's perfect textual preservation across MT, LXX, SP, and all witnesses reflects its liturgical use — recited so frequently that variation was impossible.

tx.title: Moses' shining face — qāran
tx.content: MT reads that Moses' face 'shone' (qāran, from the root q-r-n, also meaning 'horned'). The LXX correctly translates as δεδόξασται ('was glorified'). Jerome's Vulgate translated as 'cornuta esset facies' ('his face was horned'), producing Michelangelo's horned Moses statue. The SP follows MT.
tx.note: Jerome's translation is not a textual variant but a translation choice — he knew the Hebrew could mean 'horned' and chose the literal rendering. The cultural consequences were enormous and enduring.

debate.title: The Thirteen Attributes: Mercy or Mercy-and-Justice?

positions.name: Mercy predominates
positions.argument: The list opens with five mercy-attributes and closes with two justice-attributes. Jewish liturgical use emphasises mercy as the foundation of Israel's appeal to God.

positions.name: Justice limits mercy: both absolute
positions.argument: God's mercy and justice are not in tension — both are fully expressed, but only through the cross where the punishment falls on the substitute rather than the sinner.

coaching.tip: 'The LORD, the LORD, the compassionate and gracious God, slow to anger, abounding in love and faithfulness' (v. 6). God names himself by character, not just existence. Israel will recite this creed throughout Scripture. Self-revelation climaxes here.
Moses's face radiates so brightly he veils it (v. 33-35). Proximity transforms. Paul reads this as the difference between old and new covenants (2 Cor 3:7-18). Unveiled faces behold and become.
